---
pagetitle: "Chapter 2: An Introduction to R — Slides"
engine: knitr
format:
  live-revealjs:
    theme: simple
    transition: slide
    slide-number: true
    toc: false
    chalkboard: true
    footer: "STA258 | Chapter 2"
    pdf-separate-fragments: false
    html-math-method: mathjax
    code-copy: true
    code-fold: false
    webr:
      packages:
        - ggplot2
    live:
      show-solutions: true
---

# Chapter 2: An Introduction to R {.title-slide}

<style>
.reveal {
  font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
  color: #2f3437;
}

.reveal h1,
.reveal h2,
.reveal h3 {
  color: #2f3437;
}

.reveal h2 {
  border-bottom: 3px solid #00695c;
  padding-bottom: 4px;
  margin-bottom: 12px;
  font-size: 1.6em;
}

.title-slide {
  text-align: center;
}

.reveal p, .reveal li {
  font-size: 0.78em;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.defbox {
  background: #c8e6f3;
  padding: 10px 14px;
  border-left: 6px solid #00695c;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-size: 0.72em;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.examplebox {
  background: #eeeeee;
  padding: 10px 14px;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-size: 0.72em;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.thinkbox {
  background: #fff8e1;
  padding: 10px 14px;
  border-left: 6px solid #f9a825;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-size: 0.72em;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.warnbox {
  background: #ffebee;
  padding: 10px 14px;
  border-left: 6px solid #c62828;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-size: 0.72em;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
  line-height: 1.35;
}

.big {
  font-size: 1.05em;
  font-weight: bold;
  color: #00695c;
}

.footer-note {
  font-size: 0.65em;
  color: #666666;
}

.center {
  text-align: center;
}

.small-text {
  font-size: 0.72em;
}

.reveal table {
  font-size: 0.78em;
}

.defbox p, .examplebox p, .thinkbox p, .warnbox p,
.defbox li, .examplebox li, .thinkbox li, .warnbox li {
  font-size: 1em;
  margin-bottom: 4px;
}
</style>

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

STA258: Statistics with Applied Probability
University of Toronto Mississauga

![](cat.png){width="300"}

::: notes
This chapter introduces R, RStudio, and the first programming ideas needed for the course.
:::

---

## Why Learn R? {#why-r .scrollable}

<div class="center">
<span class="big">Every statistician on Earth uses code.<br>You're about to learn why.</span>
</div>

. . .

::: {.examplebox}
**Three reasons:**

- **Reproducibility** — every step is written down. Re-run it next year and get the same answer.
- **Scale** — the same script that analyzes 10 rows also analyzes 10 million.
- **Sharing** — send your code, not screenshots of your clicks.
:::

. . .

::: {.thinkbox}
This whole chapter is just enough R to do the rest of STA258. You don't need to become a software engineer.
:::

---

## Roadmap {#roadmap .scrollable}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
#### 2.1 — 2.2 Setup
- What R is (and what RStudio is)
- Installing both
- The RStudio panes
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
#### 2.3 Programming Basics
- R as a calculator
- Vectors and math functions
- Data structures
- Histograms
- Packages
:::
:::::

---

## 2.1 The Statistical Computing Language {#statistical-computing-language .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Definition — R</strong><br>
R is a programming language designed for data manipulation, statistics, graphics, and reproducible analysis.
</div>

::: {.examplebox}
**R is good at:**

- arithmetic on numbers, vectors, matrices
- producing publication-quality graphs
- using built-in and user-contributed packages
- saving entire analyses as scripts that anyone can re-run
:::

::: notes
Emphasize that R is free, open-source, and the standard tool in statistics.
:::

---

## Point-and-click vs Code {#point-vs-code .scrollable}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="examplebox">
<strong>Point-and-click software</strong><br><br>
Quick at first. But every click is invisible — hard to repeat, hard to audit, hard to share.
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>R scripts</strong><br><br>
Every step is written down. The script <em>is</em> the analysis — you can re-run it, version it, share it, or modify one line and re-run everything.
</div>
:::
:::::

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Bottom line:</strong> R makes statistical work reproducible, transparent, and automatic.
</div>

---

## 2.2 R vs RStudio {#r-vs-rstudio .scrollable}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>R</strong><br>
The programming language. The thing that does the calculations.
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>RStudio</strong><br>
The interface. Where you write code, see plots, view files, and read output.
</div>
:::
:::::

. . .

::: {.examplebox}
**Analogy:** R is the **engine**. RStudio is the **dashboard**.<br>
You can drive an engine without a dashboard — but you wouldn't want to.
:::

. . .

<div class="warnbox">
<strong>Install order matters:</strong> install <strong>R first</strong>, then RStudio on top.
</div>

---

## 2.2.1 Installing R {#install-r .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
Go to <code>cran.r-project.org</code> and pick your operating system.
</div>

::: {.examplebox}
- **Windows** → Download R for Windows → `base` → run the installer
- **macOS** → Download R for macOS → pick the matching `.pkg` file
- **Linux** → follow your distribution's instructions
:::

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
Once installed, R can run from a terminal — but in this course we always use it through RStudio.
</div>

---

## 2.2.2 Installing RStudio {#install-rstudio .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>RStudio</strong><br>
An integrated development environment (IDE) designed for working with R.
</div>

::: {.examplebox}
**RStudio gives you:**

- a script editor (write code)
- a console (run code)
- environment & history (see your objects)
- files / plots / packages / help (everything else)
:::

::: notes
Mention: RStudio is now made by Posit. Same product, new company name.
:::

---

## The Four RStudio Panes {#rstudio-panes .scrollable}

| Pane | What it does |
|:---|:---|
| **Source** | Write and edit `.R` scripts and `.qmd` files |
| **Console** | Run commands directly and see output |
| **Environment / History** | Inspect saved objects and past commands |
| **Files / Plots / Packages / Help** | Browse files, view graphs, manage packages |

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Shortcut:</strong> <code>Cmd + Enter</code> (Mac) or <code>Ctrl + Enter</code> (Windows) runs the current line.
</div>

---

## A Typical Workflow {#workflow .scrollable}

::: {.examplebox}
1. Open RStudio
2. Create a new R script (File → New File → R Script)
3. Type your code in the **Source** pane
4. Hit `Cmd/Ctrl + Enter` to run the current line
5. See results in the **Console** or **Plots** pane
6. Save the script so you can re-run later
:::

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
Saving the script is the whole point — your analysis lives in the file, not in the console.
</div>

---

## 2.2.3 Positron {#positron .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Positron</strong><br>
Another desktop environment from Posit, designed for modern data science.
</div>

. . .

::: {.examplebox}
**For STA258:** you only need RStudio. Positron is worth knowing about, but not required.
:::

---

## 2.3 Programming in R {#programming-in-r .scrollable}

R was designed for:

- data analysis
- statistical calculations
- visualization
- reproducible reporting

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Goal for this course:</strong> learn enough R to compute, summarize, visualize, and report data clearly. Nothing more.
</div>

---

## 2.3.1 R as a Calculator {#r-as-calculator .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
R handles arithmetic, exponents, logs, trig, and many other operations out of the box.
</div>

| Operation | R Code | Result |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Addition | `2 + 3` | 5 |
| Subtraction | `4 - 1` | 3 |
| Multiplication | `6 * 2` | 12 |
| Division | `8 / 2` | 4 |
| Exponentiation | `5^2` | 25 |

---

## Try It: Basic Arithmetic {#try-arithmetic .scrollable}

Edit any of the lines and run. Try `2 + 3 * 4` — what does R do first?

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

2 + 3
4 - 1
6 * 2
8 / 2
5^2
```

---

## Modulo and Integer Division {#modulo .scrollable}

| Operation | R Code | Meaning |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Modulo | `9 %% 4` | Remainder after division |
| Integer division | `9 %/% 4` | Whole-number part of division |

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

9 %% 4    # remainder: 1
9 %/% 4   # quotient: 2
```

. . .

::: {.examplebox}
Modulo is surprisingly useful — checking if a number is even (`x %% 2 == 0`), wrapping indices, etc.
:::

---

## Vectors {#vectors-intro .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Vector</strong><br>
A one-dimensional sequence of values, all of the same type. Built with <code>c()</code>.
</div>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

x <- c(1, 2, 3)
y <- c(4, 5, 6)

x + y      # element-wise
x * y      # element-wise
x^2        # square each element
```

. . .

::: {.thinkbox}
R applies operations **element-by-element**. No `for` loops needed for most arithmetic.
:::

---

## Math Functions {#math-functions .scrollable}

| Function | Meaning | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `sqrt()` | Square root | `sqrt(16)` → 4 |
| `log()` | Natural log | `log(10)` |
| `log10()` | Log base 10 | `log10(100)` → 2 |
| `exp()` | Exponential | `exp(1)` → e |
| `abs()` | Absolute value | `abs(-5)` → 5 |
| `round()` | Round to digits | `round(3.1415, 2)` → 3.14 |

---

## Try It: Math Functions {#try-math .scrollable}

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

sqrt(16)
log(10)
log10(100)
exp(1)
abs(-5)
round(3.1415, 2)
sin(pi / 2)
cos(0)
```

---

## Summary Functions {#summary-functions .scrollable}

The functions you'll use most in this course:

| Function | Returns |
|:---|:---|
| `mean(x)` | Average ($\bar{x}$) |
| `sum(x)` | Total |
| `min(x)` / `max(x)` | Smallest / largest |
| `sd(x)` | Standard deviation ($s$) |
| `var(x)` | Variance ($s^2$) |
| `length(x)` | Sample size ($n$) |

---

## Try It: Summary Functions {#try-summary .scrollable}

Change the values in `x`, then re-run.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

x <- c(10, 3, 8, 25, 7)

mean(x)
sum(x)
min(x)
max(x)
sd(x)
var(x)
length(x)
```

---

## Quick Practice: Calculate the Mean {#mean-practice .scrollable}

Fill in the function name to compute the sample mean of `x`.

```{webr}
#| exercise: mean-practice

x <- c(10, 3, 8, 25, 7)
____(x)
```

::: {.solution exercise="mean-practice"}
::: {.callout-tip title="Solution"}
```r
mean(x)   # = 10.6
```
This is $\bar{x}$.
:::
:::

---

## 2.3.2 The Six Data Structures {#data-structures .scrollable}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Vectors** — 1D, same type
- **Lists** — flexible, mixed types
- **Matrices** — 2D, same type
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Arrays** — 3D+, same type
- **Factors** — categorical
- **Data frames** — real datasets
:::
:::::

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
You'll meet all six this chapter — but in STA258 you'll spend 80% of your time with **vectors** and **data frames**.
</div>

---

## Vectors {#vectors .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
One-dimensional sequence. All elements share a type (numeric, character, logical).
</div>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

v <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
v
length(v)
v[3]          # third element
v[2:4]        # elements 2 through 4
```

---

## Lists {#lists .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
A list can hold anything — numbers, strings, vectors, even other lists. Elements can be named.
</div>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

person <- list(
  name   = "Alice",
  age    = 25,
  scores = c(90, 85, 88),
  passed = TRUE
)

person$name
person[[2]]
person[["scores"]]
```

---

## Matrices {#matrices .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
Two dimensions, all the same type. Built with <code>matrix()</code>.
</div>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

m <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), nrow = 3, ncol = 2)
m

m[1, 2]    # row 1, column 2
m[, 1]     # entire column 1
m[2, ]     # entire row 2
```

---

## Matrix Operations {#matrix-ops .scrollable}

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

m <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), nrow = 3, ncol = 2)

t(m)         # transpose
m %*% t(m)   # matrix multiplication
```

. . .

::: {.examplebox}
`%*%` is **matrix** multiplication. Plain `*` is element-wise — they are very different!
:::

---

## Arrays {#arrays .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
A multi-dimensional structure. You can think of a 3D array as a stack of matrices.
</div>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

a <- array(1:12, dim = c(2, 3, 2))
a

a[1, 2, 1]   # one element
a[, , 2]     # slice 2
```

. . .

::: {.footer-note}
Arrays are rare in intro stats — included for completeness.
:::

---

## Factors {#factors .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
A factor stores a categorical variable. R remembers which values are valid (the "levels").
</div>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

gender <- factor(c("Male", "Female", "Female", "Male"))
gender
levels(gender)
table(gender)
```

. . .

::: {.examplebox}
Factors are how R handles qualitative data — gender, education level, treatment group, etc.
:::

---

## Data Frames {#data-frames .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
A table. Each <strong>column</strong> is a variable. Each <strong>row</strong> is an observation. Columns can be different types.
</div>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

df <- data.frame(
  name   = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"),
  age    = c(25, 30, 28),
  passed = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE)
)

df
df$name      # one column
df[1, ]      # first row
df[, 2]      # second column
nrow(df); ncol(df)
```

. . .

::: {.thinkbox}
This is the structure you'll use for almost every real dataset in STA258.
:::

---

## Cheat Sheet {#data-structures-cheat .scrollable}

| Structure | Dimensions | Same type? | Use for |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| Vector | 1D | Yes | A column of values |
| List | flexible | No | Mixed information |
| Matrix | 2D | Yes | Numerical tables |
| Array | 3D+ | Yes | High-D data |
| Factor | 1D | Yes | Categorical variable |
| Data frame | 2D | No | Real datasets |

---

## 2.3.3 Functions {#functions .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
Most R commands look like <code>function_name(input)</code>.<br>
The function takes inputs and returns output.
</div>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

x <- c(10, 3, 8, 25, 7)

max(x)
min(x)
sort(x)
sort(x, decreasing = TRUE)
order(x)
```

. . .

<div class="warnbox">
<strong>Common error:</strong> missing brackets, commas, or quotes. R is unforgiving.
</div>

---

## 2.3.4 Histograms {#histograms .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Histogram</strong><br>
A graph showing the <strong>distribution</strong> of a numerical variable by grouping values into intervals (bins).
</div>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

names(faithful)   # built-in dataset: Old Faithful geyser data
head(faithful)
```

. . .

::: {.footer-note}
`faithful` records eruption length and waiting time between eruptions.
:::

---

## Try It: Make a Histogram {#try-hist .scrollable}

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

hist(faithful$waiting,
     main = "Waiting time between eruptions",
     xlab = "Minutes",
     col  = "#80cbc4")
```

. . .

::: {.examplebox}
The shape (two humps!) tells you something real about the data — Old Faithful has two distinct eruption modes.
:::

---

## Behind the Histogram {#hist-internals .scrollable}

You can grab the numbers a histogram uses:

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

h <- hist(faithful$waiting, plot = FALSE)
h$breaks      # bin edges
h$counts      # how many observations in each bin
```

. . .

::: {.thinkbox}
This is useful when you want exact counts for a report, or to compare two distributions.
:::

---

## Practice: Change the Variable {#hist-practice .scrollable}

Change the code below so it plots the histogram of `eruptions` instead of `waiting`.

```{webr}
#| exercise: histogram-practice

hist(faithful$waiting)
```

::: {.solution exercise="histogram-practice"}
::: {.callout-tip title="Solution"}
```r
hist(faithful$eruptions)
```
You should see two clear peaks — short eruptions (~2 min) and long eruptions (~4.5 min).
:::
:::

---

## 2.3.5 Packages {#packages .scrollable}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Package</strong><br>
A collection of extra functions, datasets, and tools that extend R.
</div>

::: {.examplebox}
**Install once** (downloads to your computer):
```r
install.packages("ggplot2")
```

**Load every new session** (makes it available in this R session):
```r
library(ggplot2)
```
:::

. . .

<div class="warnbox">
<strong>Common mistake:</strong> forgetting to <code>library()</code> after restarting RStudio. Installation is permanent; loading is per-session.
</div>

---

## Try It: Inspect Packages {#try-packages .scrollable}

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

# What's installed?
rownames(installed.packages())[1:8]

# Search help across all installed packages
help.search("histogram")
```

---

## Chapter 2 Summary {#chapter-2-summary .scrollable}

::: {.examplebox}
1. **R** runs the code; **RStudio** is where you write it.
2. R can do everything a calculator can — and a lot more.
3. **Vectors** and **data frames** are the structures you'll use most.
4. Most R commands are functions: `function_name(input)`.
5. **Histograms** show the shape of a numerical variable's distribution.
6. **Packages** extend R: `install.packages()` once, `library()` every session.
:::

---

## What's Next {#whats-next .scrollable}

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Chapter 3:</strong> we use these R tools to compute and visualize <strong>descriptive statistics</strong> — means, medians, spreads, and the shape of data.
</div>

<br>

<div class="center">
<span class="big">End of Chapter 2</span><br><br>
<a href="chapter2.html">← Back to Chapter 2</a>
</div>
